Chapter.title: Risk Assessment
Chapter.Article: (1) Obliged entities must identify and assess their money laundering and terrorist financing (ML/TF) risks, taking into account risk factors relating (among others) to: customers, countries or geographic areas, products, services, transactions, and delivery channels. The assessment must be proportionate to the nature and size of the entity. (2) Risk assessments must be documented, kept up to date, and made available to competent authorities or self-regulatory bodies on request. (3) Obliged entities must have in place policies, controls, and procedures to mitigate and manage effectively the ML/TF risks identified. These must be proportionate to the nature and size of the entity and must include: (a) internal policies covering: risk management, customer due diligence (CDD), reporting, record-keeping, internal control, compliance management (including, where appropriate, appointment of a compliance officer at management level), and employee screening; (b) where appropriate to the size and nature of the business, an independent audit function to test internal policies, controls, and procedures. (5) Senior management approval must be obtained for the policies, controls, and procedures put in place, and measures must be monitored and enhanced where appropriate.

AMLD4_ObligedEntity_Obligations.Chapter:
Credit institutions and financial institutions must not keep anonymous accounts or anonymous passbooks. Existing anonymous accounts must be subjected to CDD measures as soon as possible and before they are used in any way. Obliged entities must also take measures to prevent misuse of bearer shares and bearer share warrants.
Obliged entities must apply CDD measures in all of the following circumstances: (a) when establishing a business relationship; (b) when carrying out an occasional transaction of EUR 15,000 or more (single or linked operations), or a fund transfer exceeding EUR 1,000; (c) specifically for traders in goods: occasional cash transactions of EUR 10,000 or more (single or linked operations); (d) specifically for gambling service providers: transactions of EUR 2,000 or more (collection of winnings, wagering of a stake, or both); (e) whenever there is a suspicion of ML or TF, regardless of any threshold or exemption; (f) when there are doubts about the accuracy or adequacy of previously obtained customer identification data.
Obliged entities must apply all of the following CDD measures (Standard Customer Due Diligence Measures): (a) CUSTOMER IDENTIFICATION: identify the customer and verify their identity using documents, data, or information from a reliable and independent source; (b) BENEFICIAL OWNER (UBO) IDENTIFICATION: identify the beneficial owner and take reasonable measures to verify their identity; for legal persons, trusts, and similar structures, understand the ownership and control structure; (c) PURPOSE OF RELATIONSHIP: assess and, where appropriate, obtain information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship; (d) ONGOING MONITORING: continuously monitor the business relationship, scrutinise transactions to ensure consistency with the customer's known business and risk profile (including source of funds where necessary), and keep all documents and data up to date. When identifying and verifying the customer and beneficial owner, obliged entities must also verify that any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer is duly authorised, and identify and verify that person's identity. CDD measures may be applied on a risk-sensitive basis to determine their extent, but all four elements above must be applied. Obliged entities must be able to demonstrate to competent authorities that the measures taken are appropriate given the identified ML/TF risks. For life insurance and investment-related insurance: in addition to standard CDD on the customer and beneficial owner, obliged entities must also identify the beneficiaries of the policy (by name, or by sufficient information to identify them at payout). Verification of beneficiary identity must occur at the time of payout. Where the policy is assigned to a third party, the beneficial owner of the assignee must be identified at the time of assignment. For trusts and similar arrangements with class beneficiaries: sufficient information must be obtained to identify beneficiaries at the time of payout or exercise of vested rights.
(1) Verification of customer and beneficial owner identity must normally be completed BEFORE establishing a business relationship or carrying out a transaction. (2) Exceptionally, verification may be completed DURING the establishment of a business relationship if interruption would disrupt normal business and ML/TF risk is low; it must then be completed as soon as practicable. (3) For account opening at credit or financial institutions: the account may be opened before verification is complete, provided that no transactions are carried out until full CDD under Article 13(1)(a) and (b) is obtained. (4) If an obliged entity cannot complete CDD requirements (Article 13(1)(a), (b), or (c)), it must: not carry out the transaction, not establish the business relationship, terminate any existing relationship, and consider filing a suspicious transaction report (STR) with the FIU. (5) CDD must be applied not only to new customers but also to existing customers on a risk-sensitive basis, including when relevant circumstances change.
Where lower ML/TF risk is identified (taking into account at least Annex II factors), obliged entities may apply simplified CDD measures. Before applying simplified CDD, the obliged entity must ascertain that the business relationship or transaction genuinely presents lower risk. Even when applying simplified CDD, obliged entities must carry out sufficient monitoring to detect unusual or suspicious transactions.
When assessing whether simplified CDD is appropriate, obliged entities must consider at least the Annex II factors, including: CUSTOMER factors: publicly listed companies with adequate ownership disclosure; public administrations; customers resident in lower-risk geographic areas. PRODUCT/SERVICE factors: low-premium life insurance; certain pension schemes; financial inclusion products; electronic money products with purse limits and ownership transparency. GEOGRAPHIC factors: EU Member States; third countries with effective AML/CFT systems; third countries with low corruption; third countries that effectively implement FATF Recommendations.
Obliged entities MUST apply enhanced CDD (EDD) measures in all of the following situations: — when dealing with customers or beneficial owners established in high-risk third countries identified by the Commission; — in the specific cases covered by Articles 18a–24 (cross-border correspondent banking, politically exposed persons, shell banks); — in any other higher-risk situation identified by the Member State or by the obliged entity itself (considering Annex III factors). In all EDD cases, obliged entities must examine, as far as reasonably possible, the background and purpose of complex, unusually large, or unusual-pattern transactions with no apparent economic or lawful purpose, and increase the degree and intensity of monitoring of those business relationships. Annex III higher-risk indicators include: CUSTOMER: unusual business circumstances; customers in high-risk geographic areas; personal asset-holding vehicles; nominee shareholders; cash-intensive businesses; unusual or excessively complex ownership structures. PRODUCT/SERVICE: private banking; products favouring anonymity; non-face-to-face relationships without adequate safeguards; payments from unknown third parties; new or developing technologies. GEOGRAPHIC: countries without effective AML/CFT systems; countries with high corruption; sanctioned countries; countries supporting terrorism.
For ALL business relationships or transactions involving high-risk third countries identified by the Commission under Article 9(2), obliged entities MUST apply ALL of the following enhanced CDD measures: (a) obtain additional information on the customer and on the beneficial owner(s); (b) obtain additional information on the intended nature of the business relationship; (c) obtain information on the source of funds and source of wealth of the customer and of the beneficial owner(s); (d) obtain information on the reasons for the intended or performed transactions; (e) obtain senior management approval for establishing or continuing the business relationship; (f) conduct enhanced ongoing monitoring by increasing the number and frequency of controls applied, and selecting patterns of transactions that need further examination. Member States may additionally require that, where applicable, the first payment be made through an account held in the customer's name at a credit institution subject to CDD standards at least as robust as those in this Directive.
In addition to the paragraph 1 measures, where required by their Member State, obliged entities must apply one or more of the following additional mitigating measures to persons and legal entities carrying out transactions involving high-risk third countries: (a) apply additional elements of enhanced due diligence beyond those in paragraph 1; (b) introduce enhanced or systematic reporting of financial transactions to the relevant authorities; (c) limit or restrict business relationships or transactions with natural persons or legal entities from the identified high-risk third country.
Where required by their Member State, credit institutions and financial institutions must review and amend, or if necessary TERMINATE, correspondent relationships with respondent institutions located in the identified high-risk third country.
Article 18a creates a mandatory, exhaustive list of EDD measures specifically for high-risk third countries identified under Article 9(2). It operates alongside Article 18 (general enhanced CDD) but is more prescriptive: all paragraph 1 measures are compulsory, whereas Article 18 grants obliged entities greater discretion in selecting appropriate EDD measures. The paragraph 1 obligations on obliged entities are not contingent on Member State implementation choices.
For cross-border correspondent relationships with third-country respondent institutions, credit institutions and financial institutions must, in addition to standard CDD: (a) gather sufficient information to fully understand the respondent's business and assess its reputation and quality of supervision from publicly available information; (b) assess the respondent institution's AML/CFT controls; (c) obtain senior management approval before establishing new correspondent relationships; (d) document the respective AML/CFT responsibilities of each institution; (e) for payable-through accounts: obtain satisfaction that the respondent has verified the identity of, and performs ongoing due diligence on, customers with direct access, and can provide relevant CDD data on request.
xml
Crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) must identify and assess the ML/TF risk associated with transfers of crypto-assets directed to or originating from a SELF-HOSTED ADDRESS (i.e. a crypto-asset address not held by a regulated CASP or financial institution).
To that end, CASPs must have in place internal policies, procedures, and controls specifically covering self-hosted address transfers.
CASPs must apply mitigating measures commensurate with the risks identified. Those measures must include one or more of the following: (a) IDENTIFICATION: take risk-based measures to identify and verify the identity of the originator or beneficiary of the transfer to/from the self-hosted address, or the beneficial owner of such originator or beneficiary — including through reliance on third parties (Article 25); (b) ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: require additional information on the origin and destination of the transferred crypto-assets; (c) ENHANCED MONITORING: conduct enhanced ongoing monitoring of those transactions; (d) OTHER MEASURES: apply any other measure to mitigate and manage ML/TF risks as well as the risk of non-implementation or evasion of targeted financial sanctions (TFS) and proliferation-financing-related TFS.
Note: The selection of measures must be proportionate to the risk level identified for each self-hosted address transfer. EBA guidelines (due by 30 December 2024) will specify the criteria and means for identification and verification under this Article.
For cross-border CORRESPONDENT RELATIONSHIPS involving the execution of crypto-asset services with a respondent entity NOT established in the EU and providing similar services (including crypto-asset transfers), CASPs must apply the following measures IN ADDITION to standard CDD under Article 13, when entering into a business relationship with such an entity:
(a) LICENSING CHECK: determine whether the respondent entity is licensed or registered in its jurisdiction; (b) BUSINESS UNDERSTANDING: gather sufficient information to fully understand the nature of the respondent's business and assess, from publicly available information, its reputation and the quality of supervision it is subject to; (c) AML/CFT ASSESSMENT: assess the respondent entity's AML/CFT controls; (d) SENIOR MANAGEMENT APPROVAL: obtain senior management approval before establishing any new correspondent relationship; (e) RESPONSIBILITY DOCUMENTATION: document the respective AML/CFT responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship; (f) PAYABLE-THROUGH ACCOUNTS: where the arrangement involves payable-through crypto-asset accounts, obtain satisfaction that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performs ongoing due diligence on, customers with direct access to the correspondent entity's accounts, and that the respondent can provide relevant CDD data to the correspondent upon request.
TERMINATION: Where a CASP decides to terminate a correspondent relationship for AML/CFT policy reasons, it must document and record the decision and the grounds for it.
ONGOING UPDATES: CASPs must update their due diligence information for correspondent relationships on a regular basis and whenever new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.
CASPs must use the information gathered under paragraph 1 to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate additional mitigating measures to address the risks associated with the respondent entity. Note: EBA guidelines (due by 30 June 2024) will specify the assessment criteria and risk-mitigating measures under this Article, including minimum action where the respondent entity is unlicensed or unregistered.
Article 19b is the crypto-asset equivalent of Article 19 (cross-border correspondent banking). The key structural differences are: — CASPs must additionally verify whether the respondent is licensed or registered (paragraph 1(a)), which has no direct equivalent in Article 19; — CASPs must document termination decisions made on AML/CFT grounds (no equivalent in Article 19); — CASPs must keep due diligence information current on a regular basis and when new risks emerge (Article 19 implies but does not state this explicitly). The scope excludes custody and administration of crypto-assets on behalf of clients (MiCA Article 3(1)(16)(h)), meaning pure custody providers are not subject to this article's correspondent relationship regime.
For transactions or business relationships with politically exposed persons (PEPs), obliged entities must, in addition to standard CDD: (a) have in place risk-based procedures to determine whether the customer or beneficial owner is a PEP; (b) obtain senior management approval before establishing or continuing a business relationship with a PEP; (c) take adequate measures to establish the source of wealth and source of funds involved in the business relationship or transaction; (d) conduct enhanced, ongoing monitoring of the business relationship. PEP definition includes: heads of State/government, ministers, members of parliament, members of governing bodies of political parties, judges of supreme/constitutional courts, members of courts of auditors or central bank boards, ambassadors and senior military officers, directors of State-owned enterprises, and senior officials of international organisations. Middle-ranking and junior officials are excluded.
For life and investment-related insurance, obliged entities must take reasonable measures to determine whether policy beneficiaries (or their beneficial owners) are PEPs. This check must be completed no later than at the time of payout or assignment. Where higher risk is identified, obliged entities must: (a) inform senior management before paying out policy proceeds; (b) conduct enhanced scrutiny of the entire business relationship with the policyholder.
When a person ceases to hold a prominent public function, obliged entities must continue to apply appropriate and risk-sensitive EDD measures for at least 12 months, until the person is deemed to pose no further PEP-specific risk.
The EDD measures required for PEPs under Articles 20 and 21 must also be applied to family members and persons known to be close associates of PEPs. Family members include: spouse or equivalent, children and their spouses, and parents. Close associates include persons with joint beneficial ownership of legal entities/arrangements with the PEP, or persons who hold beneficial ownership of a legal entity/arrangement set up for the de facto benefit of the PEP.
Credit institutions and financial institutions must not enter into or continue correspondent relationships with shell banks (i.e., credit or financial institutions incorporated in a jurisdiction with no physical presence, meaningful mind and management, and no affiliation to a regulated financial group). They must also take appropriate measures to ensure they do not engage in or continue correspondent relationships with any institution that is known to allow its accounts to be used by a shell bank.
Obliged entities may rely on third parties to carry out CDD requirements under Article 13(1)(a), (b), and (c) (customer identification, beneficial owner identification, and purpose of relationship). However, the ULTIMATE RESPONSIBILITY for meeting CDD requirements always remains with the obliged entity that relies on the third party.
An entity may only be relied upon as a third party for CDD if it: (a) applies CDD and record-keeping requirements consistent with this Directive; and (b) has its compliance with those requirements supervised in a manner consistent with this Directive. Obliged entities may NOT rely on third parties established in high-risk third countries identified by the Commission.
When relying on a third party, the obliged entity must: (a) obtain from the third party all necessary information concerning the CDD requirements under Article 13(1)(a), (b), and (c); (b) take adequate steps to ensure that the third party will immediately provide, upon request, relevant copies of identification and verification data and other documentation on the identity of the customer or beneficial owner.

Chapter.title: Beneficial Ownership Information
Chapter.Article: Corporate and other legal entities must obtain and hold adequate, accurate, and current information on their beneficial ownership, including details of the beneficial interests held. They must provide this information to obliged entities when CDD measures are being applied. For obliged entities conducting CDD: do not rely exclusively on the central register to fulfil CDD requirements; use a risk-based approach. Beneficial owner definition for corporate entities: the natural person(s) who ultimately own or control the entity through direct or indirect ownership of more than 25% of shares/voting rights, or through other means of control. If no such person can be identified after exhausting all means, the senior managing official(s) may be treated as the beneficial owner(s), with records kept of the steps taken.

Trustees must obtain and hold adequate, accurate, and up-to-date beneficial ownership information on the trust, covering: the settlor, trustee(s), protector (if any), beneficiaries or class of beneficiaries, and any other person exercising effective control over the trust. Trustees must disclose their status as trustee and provide the above information to obliged entities in a timely manner when forming a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction above the relevant thresholds. This obligation applies equally to other legal arrangements with structures or functions similar to trusts.

Chapter.title: Reporting Obligations
Chapter.Article: Obliged entities, their directors, and employees must: (a) PROACTIVE REPORTING: inform the FIU on their own initiative, by filing a suspicious transaction report (STR), where the entity knows, suspects, or has reasonable grounds to suspect that funds (regardless of amount) are proceeds of criminal activity or are related to terrorist financing; and promptly respond to requests by the FIU for additional information; (b) REACTIVE REPORTING: provide the FIU, directly or indirectly and upon request, with all necessary information in accordance with applicable law. All suspicious transactions, INCLUDING ATTEMPTED TRANSACTIONS, must be reported. The compliance officer (appointed under Article 8(4)(a)) is responsible for transmitting the report to the FIU of the Member State where the entity is established.

Article.title: Obligation to Refrain from Executing Suspicious Transactions

Chapter.Article: Obliged entities must refrain from executing transactions they know or suspect to be related to ML or TF until they have filed an STR under Article 33(1)(a) and complied with any further instructions from the FIU or competent authorities. If refraining is impossible or would frustrate efforts to pursue beneficiaries, the FIU must be informed immediately afterwards.

Disclosure of information in good faith by an obliged entity, or by its employees or directors, in accordance with Articles 33 and 34 does not constitute a breach of any contractual, legislative, regulatory, or administrative restriction on disclosure, and does not give rise to any liability, even if the underlying criminal activity was not precisely known or did not actually occur.

Obliged entities must ensure that employees and representatives who report suspicions of ML or TF internally or to the FIU are protected from threats, hostile action, and adverse or discriminatory employment actions.

Obliged entities, their directors, and employees must NOT disclose to the customer or any third party that an STR has been, is being, or will be filed, or that a ML/TF analysis is underway or may be carried out. Exceptions (disclosure is permitted): — to competent authorities or self-regulatory bodies, or for law enforcement purposes; — within a group (between credit/financial institutions and their group branches/subsidiaries in third countries), provided group-wide AML/CFT policies under Article 45 are fully complied with; — between obliged entities within the same legal person or structure sharing common ownership, management, or compliance control; — between obliged entities from the same professional category, dealing with the same customer and the same transaction, subject to equivalent AML/CFT and professional secrecy obligations.

Chapter.title: Data Protection, Record-Keeping, and Statistics
Chapter.Article: Obliged entities must retain the following for FIVE YEARS after the end of the business relationship or the date of an occasional transaction: (a) CDD records: copies of documents and information used to comply with CDD requirements under Chapter II; (b) Transaction records: supporting evidence and transaction records (original documents or admissible copies) sufficient to identify each transaction. Upon expiry of the five-year period, personal data must be deleted unless national law provides otherwise. Retention may be extended for a further period not exceeding five additional years, if a thorough necessity and proportionality assessment justifies it for ML/TF prevention, detection, or investigation purposes.

Article.title: Data Protection — Limits on Use of Personal Data

Chapter.Article: Obliged entities may process personal data under this Directive ONLY for the purpose of preventing ML and TF as referred to in Article 1. Further processing of that data for any other purpose, including commercial purposes, is strictly prohibited. Before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction, obliged entities must inform new clients of the legal obligations under this Directive to process their personal data for AML/CFT purposes.

Article.title: Systems for Responding to FIU Enquiries

Chapter.Article: Obliged entities must have in place systems that enable them to respond fully and promptly, through secure and confidential channels, to enquiries from the FIU or other competent authorities as to whether they are maintaining, or have maintained within the previous five years, a business relationship with a specified person, and on the nature of that relationship.

The processing of personal data for ML/TF prevention under this Directive is to be treated as a matter of public interest. Obliged entities may restrict a data subject's right of access to personal data to the extent necessary to enable the entity or competent authority to fulfil its AML/CFT functions or to avoid obstructing official investigations or analyses.

Chapter.title: Internal Policies, Training, and Group Requirements
Chapter.Article: Obliged entities that are part of a group must implement group-wide policies and procedures that include: — data protection policies; — procedures for sharing information within the group for AML/CFT purposes; — the requirement that these policies be effectively implemented at all branches and majority-owned subsidiaries in Member States and third countries. Where a branch or subsidiary is in a third country with less strict AML/CFT requirements, the group must apply the home Member State's higher standards to the extent permitted by local law. Where local law does not permit full implementation, the obliged entity must apply additional measures to manage the residual risk and inform the home competent authority. The sharing of ML/TF suspicion information within the group is permitted (and may be required), unless the FIU instructs otherwise.

Annex.title: CDD Risk Variables (Article 13(3))
Annex.usage: Obliged entities must consider these variables when determining the extent of CDD measures to apply.

Annex.title: Potentially Lower-Risk Factors (Articles 15–16)
Annex.usage: Obliged entities may apply simplified CDD where these factors indicate genuinely lower ML/TF risk.

Annexes.Annex: CUSTOMER factors: publicly listed companies with adequate ownership disclosure; public administrations; customers in lower-risk geographic areas. PRODUCT/SERVICE factors: low-premium life insurance; certain non-assignable pension schemes; financial inclusion products; electronic money with purse limits and ownership transparency. GEOGRAPHIC factors: EU Member States; third countries with effective AML/CFT systems; third countries with low corruption; third countries that effectively implement the revised FATF Recommendations.

Annex.title: Potentially Higher-Risk Factors (Article 18(3))
Annex.usage: Obliged entities must apply enhanced CDD where these factors indicate higher ML/TF risk.

Annexes.Annex: CUSTOMER factors: unusual business circumstances; customers in high-risk geographic areas; personal asset-holding vehicles; companies with nominee shareholders or bearer shares; cash-intensive businesses; unusual or excessively complex ownership structures. PRODUCT/SERVICE factors: private banking; products favouring anonymity; non-face-to-face relationships without adequate safeguards; payments from unknown or unassociated third parties; new products, delivery mechanisms, or technologies. GEOGRAPHIC factors: countries without effective AML/CFT systems; high corruption countries; countries under UN/EU sanctions or embargoes; countries providing funding or support for terrorist activities.

Ref.summary: Conduct, document, and update ML/TF risk assessments; maintain proportionate policies, controls, and procedures; obtain senior management approval
Implement group-wide AML/CFT policies at all branches and subsidiaries
Train employees; identify the management-board AML/CFT responsible person
No anonymous accounts; prevent bearer share misuse
Apply CDD on business relationship establishment, transactions above thresholds, and upon suspicion
Core CDD: identify customer, identify beneficial owner, assess relationship purpose, conduct ongoing monitoring
Timing of CDD: verify before relationship/transaction; inability to complete CDD → do not proceed, consider STR
Simplified CDD permitted where lower risk is demonstrated; ongoing monitoring still required
Consider Annex II factors when assessing eligibility for simplified CDD
Enhanced CDD mandatory for high-risk third countries and other higher-risk situations (Annex III)

For ALL business relationships/transactions involving Commission-listed high-risk third countries: mandatory application of all six paragraph 1 EDD measures (additional customer/UBO information, relationship purpose, source of funds/wealth, transaction reasons, senior management approval, enhanced monitoring); plus one or more additional Member-State-required mitigating measures (paragraph 2); credit and financial institutions may additionally be required to review, amend, or terminate correspondent relationships with respondents in those countries (paragraph 3(e))
Additional EDD for cross-border correspondent banking with third-country respondents
Additional EDD for PEPs: risk procedures, senior management approval, source of wealth/funds, enhanced monitoring
Check insurance beneficiaries for PEP status; senior management notification and enhanced scrutiny if higher risk
Maintain EDD for former PEPs for at least 12 months after leaving public function
Apply PEP EDD measures to PEP family members and close associates
Prohibition: no correspondent relationships with shell banks

Identify and assess ML/TF risk of transfers to/from self-hosted addresses; maintain internal policies and controls; apply commensurate mitigating measures (risk-based identification of originator/beneficiary, additional information on origin/destination, enhanced monitoring, or other TFS/proliferation-financing measures)

For cross-border correspondent relationships with non-EU crypto-asset service providers: verify licensing status; assess reputation, supervision quality, and AML/CFT controls; obtain senior management approval; document responsibilities; apply payable-through account safeguards; document AML/CFT-driven termination decisions; update due diligence regularly and when new risks emerge; apply risk-sensitive mitigating measures based on the assessment
May rely on eligible third parties for CDD; ultimate responsibility remains with the obliged entity
Third parties must meet AML/CFT standards; cannot rely on third parties in high-risk third countries
Must obtain all necessary CDD information from the third party; must be able to obtain documents immediately on request
Legal entities must hold accurate beneficial ownership information and provide it to obliged entities during CDD; do not rely solely on central register
Trustees must hold and disclose beneficial ownership information; applies also to trust-like arrangements
File STRs proactively with the FIU on suspicion of ML/TF; respond to FIU information requests; report all suspicious and attempted transactions
Refrain from executing suspicious transactions until STR is filed and FIU instructions are received
Good-faith reporting is legally protected
Protect whistleblowers from retaliation
Do not tip off the customer or third parties that an STR has been filed or that a ML/TF investigation is underway
Retain CDD records and transaction records for 5 years; delete personal data on expiry unless extended retention is justified
Process personal data only for AML/CFT purposes; no commercial reuse; inform new clients of data processing obligations
Maintain systems to respond promptly and securely to FIU enquiries about business relationships within the past 5 years
AML/CFT data processing is a public-interest matter; access rights of data subjects may be restricted as necessary
